topic: Key Gases & Fuels — Hydrogen, Oxygen, Nitrogen, CO₂, Ozone, LPG & CNG

questions.text: Which gas is used in fire extinguishers to put out fires, and why is it effective?
options: Oxygen (O₂) — it dilutes the fuel so it cannot burn
Nitrogen (N₂) — because it reacts chemically with the fire to neutralise it
Carbon dioxide (CO₂) — it is heavier than air, does not support combustion, and smothers the fire by cutting off the oxygen supply
Hydrogen (H₂) — it absorbs the heat of the fire very quickly, cooling it below ignition temperature
questions.explanation: CO₂ fire extinguishers work because carbon dioxide: (1) Does NOT support combustion — CO₂ is already fully oxidised (carbon has combined with 2 oxygen atoms) and cannot react with more oxygen; (2) Is heavier than air (density 1.98 g/L vs air 1.29 g/L; ~1.5× heavier) — sinks and forms a blanket over the burning material, displacing the oxygen needed for combustion; (3) Smothers the fire by reducing O₂ concentration below ~16% (normal air = 21% O₂); below ~12% O₂, most fires cannot sustain. CO₂ extinguishers are ideal for: Electrical fires (CO₂ leaves no residue, is non-conductive — safe on live electrical equipment); Oil/flammable liquid fires (CO₂ blankets the surface); Computer/server room fires. CO₂ extinguishers are NOT suitable for: Class A fires (paper, wood, textiles) — CO₂ doesn't penetrate deeply; Metals (Mg, Na burn in CO₂ — use dry sand); Pressurised liquid fires (CO₂ pressure can spread the burning liquid). Key SSC fact: CO₂ cylinders feel very cold when discharged — CO₂ expands from liquid (high pressure inside) to gas → Joule-Thomson cooling → very cold gas. This also helps cool the fire. Dry ice (solid CO₂) effect sometimes visible.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years — CO₂ used in fire extinguishers (heavier than air, doesn't support combustion) is very frequently asked

questions.text: What is the main component of CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) used as fuel in vehicles?
options: Butane (C₄H₁₀)
Propane (C₃H₈)
Ethane (C₂H₆)
Methane (CH₄)
questions.explanation: CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) is composed primarily of METHANE (CH₄) — typically 85–95% methane, with small amounts of ethane, propane, butane, and nitrogen. Methane (CH₄): Simplest alkane; molecular formula = 1 carbon + 4 hydrogen; boiling point = −161.5°C (very low — stays as gas at room temperature even under high pressure); Burns cleanly: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. CNG details: Stored at 200–250 bar in strong steel/composite cylinders (as compressed gas, NOT liquid — unlike LPG which liquefies); Must NOT be confused with LPG: LPG = propane + butane (C₃ + C₄); stored as LIQUID at moderate pressure; CNG = methane (C₁); stored as COMPRESSED GAS. Advantages: ~25% lower CO₂ than petrol; negligible SO₂; fewer particulates → cleaner air; High octane number (~120) → smooth engine performance; Cheaper per km (India). Uses in India: Buses, autorickshaws, taxis, private cars; Delhi mandatory CNG for public transport from 2001 (after Supreme Court order) → dramatic improvement in Delhi's air quality. Biogas ≈ CNG when methane is separated and purified → called 'biomethane' or 'CBG' (Compressed Biogas).
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years — CNG is mainly methane (CH₄) is very frequently asked

questions.text: The Montreal Protocol (1987) was signed to protect the ozone layer. Which chemicals does it aim to phase out?
options: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) and methane (CH₄) — the main greenhouse gases
Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) and nitrogen oxides (NOₓ) — the main acid rain gases
Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and other ozone-depleting substances — used in refrigerators, aerosols, and air conditioners
Carbon monoxide (CO) and benzene — urban air pollutants from vehicle exhaust
questions.explanation: Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer (1987): Target: Phase out Ozone-Depleting Substances (ODS), primarily Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs). What are CFCs? Chlorofluorocarbons: Compounds containing C, Cl, and F (e.g., CFC-11 = CCl₃F, CFC-12 = CCl₂F₂, Freon-22 = CHClF₂); Previously used in: Refrigerators and air conditioners (refrigerant), aerosol propellants (deodorants, hairspray), foam blowing agents (Styrofoam). How CFCs destroy ozone: UV breaks the C-Cl bond in the stratosphere → Cl (atomic chlorine); Cl + O₃ → ClO + O₂ (ozone destroyed); ClO + O → Cl + O₂ (Cl regenerated — catalytic cycle); 1 Cl atom destroys up to 100,000 O₃ molecules. Also under Montreal Protocol: HCFCs (hydrochlorofluorocarbons — transitional replacements for CFCs, still slightly ozone-depleting); Halons (fire suppression chemicals, very potent ODS). Montreal Protocol achievements: Most successful environmental treaty; signed by 197 countries (every UN member); CFC production largely eliminated; ozone hole is slowly recovering (expected full recovery by 2060–2070). The Kigali Amendment (2016) to Montreal Protocol added HFCs (which replaced CFCs but are powerful greenhouse gases) to the phase-down schedule.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years — Montreal Protocol (1987) phases out CFCs to protect ozone layer is very frequently asked

questions.text: What test is used to confirm the presence of carbon dioxide gas?
options: Pass the gas through a solution of potassium permanganate — it turns colourless if CO₂ is present
Burn the gas — if it burns with a blue flame, it is CO₂
Pass the gas through limewater [Ca(OH)₂ solution] — CO₂ turns limewater milky white by forming calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) precipitate
Dip a burning splint into the gas — if the flame goes out, CO₂ is confirmed
questions.explanation: Limewater test for CO₂: Reaction: CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → CaCO₃↓ (white precipitate) + H₂O; CaCO₃ is insoluble in water → forms white, milky turbidity in the clear limewater solution. This is the DEFINITIVE laboratory test for CO₂. Process: Bubble the unknown gas through a test tube of clear limewater (dilute calcium hydroxide solution); If white cloudiness/precipitate forms → CO₂ is present. Additional information: Excess CO₂ clears the cloudiness: CaCO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → Ca(HCO₃)₂ (calcium bicarbonate, soluble → solution becomes clear again); So: if excess gas is bubbled, clear → milky → clear again = CO₂ confirmed. Option D (burning splint test): A glowing (not burning) splint relights in O₂; CO₂ extinguishes a burning splint (but so do many other gases) — NOT specific to CO₂. Option A: KMnO₄ is decolorised by reducing gases (SO₂, H₂S, H₂) — NOT CO₂ specific. CO₂ does NOT decolorise KMnO₄. Option B: CO₂ does NOT burn at all (already fully oxidised); it smothers flames. The limewater test is uniquely specific and easy to perform — the only answer.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL previous years — limewater test (milky with CaCO₃) confirms CO₂ is very frequently asked

questions.text: Ozone is described as both 'good' and 'bad' depending on where it is found. Explain the difference.
options: Ozone is only ever harmful; the description of 'good ozone' in the stratosphere is a misconception — all ozone causes respiratory problems

Stratospheric ozone (15–35 km altitude) = 'good ozone' — absorbs harmful UV-B and UV-C radiation from the sun, protecting all life on Earth. Tropospheric ozone (0–10 km, ground level) = 'bad ozone' — formed from NOₓ + VOC + sunlight reactions; a major air pollutant that damages lungs, irritates eyes, and damages crops and rubber.
Ozone in cities is 'good' because it cleans the air; ozone in forests is 'bad' because it causes trees to grow abnormally fast, displacing native species
The distinction is based on concentration: dilute ozone (< 0.01 ppm) in the stratosphere is 'good'; concentrated ozone (> 1 ppm) at ground level is 'bad'
questions.explanation: The ozone paradox — same molecule, opposite effects depending on location: GOOD OZONE — Stratospheric (15–35 km altitude): Formed naturally: UV + O₂ → 2O; O + O₂ → O₃; The ozone layer absorbs 97–99% of UV-B (290–315 nm) and all UV-C (100–280 nm); UV-B causes: skin cancer (melanoma), cataracts (eye), DNA damage, immune suppression; UV-C would be lethal to surface life — ozone completely absorbs it; Without the ozone layer, most surface life on Earth could not exist; Total ozone above a spot measured in Dobson Units (DU); Ozone hole = DU < 220 over Antarctica in spring. BAD OZONE — Tropospheric (0–10 km, especially at surface): Formed by photochemical reaction: NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight → O₃ (photochemical smog); NOₓ from vehicle exhaust and industry; VOCs from vehicles, paint, solvents, petrol vapour; Effects: Respiratory irritant (causes asthma, bronchitis); Eye and nose irritation; Damages crops and forest vegetation (reduces photosynthesis); Degrades rubber (ozone cracking in tyres and seals); Part of photochemical smog (LA Smog, visible brown haze in cities). Key distinction for SSC: Location is everything — stratosphere = good; troposphere = bad. Same O₃ molecule, very different context and consequences.
questions.source: SSC CGL — stratospheric ozone (good, UV shield) vs tropospheric ozone (bad, air pollutant) is a tricky distinction question

questions.text: Why does liquid nitrogen (LN₂) have so many seemingly magical applications in medicine and food?
options: Liquid nitrogen is not actually used in medicine or food — only in industrial cooling of metal parts; medical applications are restricted due to nitrogen's toxicity in liquid form

Liquid nitrogen boils at −196°C (77 K), the second-lowest-boiling cryogenic fluid after liquid helium. This ultra-low temperature is biologically relevant: cells and microbes are instantly killed (cryotherapy), biological samples are preserved indefinitely (biobanking), and food is flash-frozen without forming large ice crystals that damage cell walls (preserves texture and nutrients).
Liquid nitrogen has special antibacterial properties that come from nitrogen's chemical reactivity at very low temperatures; it kills bacteria by chemical reaction, not by freezing them
Liquid nitrogen works in medicine only because it is always contaminated with liquid oxygen (LN₂ is a mixture); the oxygen component provides the therapeutic benefit
questions.explanation: Liquid nitrogen (LN₂) — properties and applications: Physical properties: Boiling point: −195.79°C (77.36 K); Colourless, cryogenic liquid; Very low viscosity (flows like water); 1 litre of LN₂ expands to ~700 litres of N₂ gas at room temperature (DANGER — rapid expansion in enclosed spaces → asphyxiation). Medical applications (cryotherapy): Dermatology: Skin lesions, warts, pre-cancerous cells (actinic keratosis), seborrhoeic keratosis → directly applied LN₂ or spray → instant freezing → cells burst (ice crystal formation inside cells → lethal) → dead tissue sloughs off; Gynaecology: Cervical cryotherapy for CIN (cervical intraepithelial neoplasia); Prostate cryotherapy; Cryosurgery for tumour ablation. Biobanking (long-term preservation): Sperm, eggs (oocytes), embryos: stored in LN₂ vapour (−150°C) or liquid (−196°C) → metabolism essentially stops → preserved for decades; Stem cells, bone marrow, cord blood; Tissue samples and pathology specimens. Food industry (cryogenic freezing): Flash-freezing: food lowered into or sprayed with LN₂ → freezes in seconds; Small ice crystals form (rapid freezing) → cell walls intact → texture preserved (vs slow freezing → large ice crystals → mushy texture when thawed); Used for delicate items: berries, shrimp, peas, cooked meals. Restaurant 'molecular gastronomy': LN₂ used at table for instant frozen desserts, cocktail fog effects (nitrogen fog = condensed atmospheric moisture around cold gas).
questions.source: SSC CGL — liquid nitrogen (−196°C) uses in cryotherapy, biobanking, food freezing is a tricky applications question

questions.text: What is 'water gas' and how does it differ from natural gas and biogas?
options: Water gas is natural gas dissolved in water at high pressure; when pressure is released, the gas escapes (like opening a soda bottle)

Water gas is a synthetic mixture of carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂) made by passing steam over hot coke: C + H₂O → CO + H₂. Unlike natural gas (mainly CH₄) or biogas (CH₄ + CO₂ from biological decomposition), water gas is made industrially from coal/coke and water — NOT from biological sources.
Water gas is the gas produced when water is electrolysed: oxygen and hydrogen are produced and collectively called water gas
Water gas is the steam (water vapour) produced when fuels burn; it is considered a gas because it is in gaseous form at the high temperatures of combustion
questions.explanation: Water gas — a crucial industrial gas from the 19th–20th centuries: Formation: C (coke/coal, heated to ~1000°C) + H₂O (steam) → CO + H₂ (water gas); Composition: ~50% CO + ~50% H₂ (approximately); Also called: synthesis gas or syngas (when mixed with other components). Properties: Combustible (both CO and H₂ burn); High calorific value (~11 MJ/m³); Used as fuel and for hydrogen production; 'Producer gas' is different: C + O₂ (limited) + N₂ → CO + N₂ (burning carbon in limited air → mostly CO and N₂; lower calorific value than water gas because of diluting N₂). Comparison of gas fuels: Natural gas (CNG/PNG): Found underground; mainly CH₄; no CO; piped from wells; used directly as fuel; Biogas: CH₄ + CO₂; from biological decomposition of organic waste; renewable; Water gas: CO + H₂; from coke + steam (industrial process); man-made; historical importance as town gas before natural gas pipelines. Modern relevance: 'Syngas' (CO + H₂) produced from natural gas reforming or coal gasification → used in Fischer-Tropsch synthesis (making synthetic petrol from coal/gas) and for making ammonia (H₂ from syngas → Haber Process). Water gas is NOT suitable for domestic use without purification because CO is odourless and highly toxic.
questions.source: SSC CGL — water gas (CO + H₂, from C + H₂O) vs natural gas vs biogas is a tricky comparison question

questions.text: What is the 'hydrogen economy' concept, and what is 'green hydrogen' vs 'grey hydrogen'?
options: The hydrogen economy is a theoretical concept that has been abandoned because hydrogen cannot be stored safely

The hydrogen economy envisions hydrogen as the primary clean energy carrier. Grey hydrogen is made from fossil fuels (cheap but releases CO₂); blue hydrogen is grey + carbon capture; green hydrogen is made by electrolysis using 100% renewable electricity (zero CO₂ — the cleanest; currently expensive). India's National Hydrogen Mission targets 5 Mt of green hydrogen by 2030.
The hydrogen economy refers to the global trade in hydrogen-rich compounds (water, ammonia, methane); hydrogen itself is not traded because it is too light
Grey hydrogen is hydrogen from coal gasification (high CO₂); green hydrogen is hydrogen from natural gas (medium CO₂); the 'green' refers only to the colour of the gas at different purities
questions.explanation: Hydrogen colours — a key classification in energy policy: GREY HYDROGEN (most common, ~95% of global production): From steam methane reforming (SMR): CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (Ni catalyst, 700–1000°C); CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (water-gas shift reaction); Releases CO₂ to atmosphere (~10 kg CO₂ per kg H₂); Cheapest form (~$1–2/kg); Used in fertiliser production, oil refining. BLUE HYDROGEN: Same SMR process, but CO₂ is captured and stored (Carbon Capture and Storage, CCS); Lower CO₂ emissions but CCS is expensive and not yet proven at scale; ~$2–4/kg. GREEN HYDROGEN (the goal): Electrolysis of water: 2H₂O + electricity → 2H₂ + O₂; Using electricity from wind, solar, or hydro (renewable) → zero CO₂; Currently expensive (~$4–8/kg) due to cost of renewable electricity and electrolysers; Cost falling rapidly as renewable electricity cost drops. Fuel cell vehicles: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo — use H₂ in fuel cells: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O + electricity → drives electric motors; Zero tailpipe emissions. India National Hydrogen Mission (2021): Target 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen production by 2030; India has abundant solar and wind potential for green hydrogen production; Export target — 'hydrogen hub' for Asian market.
questions.source: SSC CGL — green vs grey hydrogen and the hydrogen economy/National Hydrogen Mission is a tricky question

questions.text: Nitrogen is used in food packaging (chips, biscuits, nuts). Why nitrogen specifically — and not just any inert gas?
options: Nitrogen adds a mild flavour to snacks that enhances the taste; it is used for flavouring, not preservation

Nitrogen is cheap (78% of air), non-toxic, dry, chemically inert, and does not react with oils or food. Replacing air in packaging with N₂ prevents oxidation (rancidity) of oils/fats and fungal/bacterial spoilage. It also keeps packaging inflated to cushion delicate snacks during transport.
Nitrogen reacts with the potato starch in chips to form a preservation compound that prevents spoilage at the molecular level
Nitrogen's extremely low boiling point (−196°C) keeps the chips cold inside the packet, preventing microbial growth
questions.explanation: Modified atmosphere packaging (MAP) with nitrogen: Why air is bad for packaged snacks: O₂ in air reacts with unsaturated fats/oils in snacks → lipid oxidation → rancidity (off-taste, off-smell; 'stale' flavour); Moisture in air + O₂ support mould/bacteria growth; CO₂ in air and O₂ can dissolve in the oils. Why N₂ is the solution: Chemistry: N₂ is completely inert with food molecules at room temperature; N₂ does not react with fats, proteins, or carbohydrates; N₂ is dry (no moisture to contribute to spoilage); Economics: N₂ is extracted from air very cheaply (separation from 78% N₂ air); About 10× cheaper than helium or argon; Safety: N₂ is completely non-toxic, tasteless, odourless — safe for food contact. Additional benefit — cushioning: Nitrogen-filled packages (chips bags, nut bags) are 'inflated' with N₂; This puffed structure cushions the delicate contents from crushing during transport and handling; The gas volume acts as a shock absorber. Other MAP gases: CO₂ (alone or with N₂): inhibits bacterial/mould growth directly; slightly acidic in moist environments; used in some fresh meat packaging; O₂ (sometimes added for fresh red meat to maintain bright red myoglobin colour and for fresh produce to support respiration).
questions.source: SSC CGL — nitrogen in food packaging (inert, prevents oxidation/rancidity, cheap, cushions) is underrated but important

questions.text: What is 'calorific value' of a fuel, and why does hydrogen have the highest calorific value despite being very lightweight?
options: Calorific value is the temperature at which a fuel ignites; hydrogen has the lowest ignition temperature, which SSC textbooks incorrectly label as the highest calorific value

Calorific value is the heat energy released per unit mass (or volume) when a fuel is completely burned. Hydrogen (H₂) has the highest calorific value (~143 kJ/g) because: the H-H bond is weak (436 kJ/mol) but the O-H bonds formed in water are very strong (2 × 497 kJ/mol = 994 kJ/mol per H₂ molecule) — the net energy release is enormous per unit mass, especially because hydrogen is so light.
Calorific value measures how much carbon dioxide is produced per litre of fuel burned; hydrogen produces the least CO₂, giving it the 'highest' calorific value by this definition
Calorific value is the energy stored per unit volume, not per unit mass; hydrogen has the lowest value by volume (because it is a gas at low pressure) and is only highest by mass
questions.explanation: Calorific value (CV) = heat released per unit mass on complete combustion in oxygen (units: kJ/g or MJ/kg). Hydrogen's record: H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O; ΔH = −286 kJ/mol per mole of H₂; Molar mass of H₂ = 2 g/mol; CV = 286/2 = ~143 kJ/g. Why so high per gram: Hydrogen atom is the LIGHTEST atom (mass 1 u); But each H-H bond breaking + 2(O-H) bond forming releases about 286 kJ; Compared to methane: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O; ΔH = −890 kJ/mol; molar mass = 16 g/mol; CV = 890/16 ≈ 55.6 kJ/g; Compared to octane (petrol): ~45 kJ/g. Calorific value order (kJ/g): H₂ (143) > Methane/CNG (55) > LPG/butane (49) > Petrol (45) > Diesel (44) > Kerosene (43) > Coal (28–35) > Wood (17) > Dung cake (7). Practical challenge of hydrogen: Very high CV per MASS but very low energy per VOLUME (because H₂ is so light); 1 kg H₂ (compressed to 350 bar in tank) has energy of ~45 litres of petrol by mass equivalent, but takes a large, heavy tank; Engineering the storage is the key challenge for hydrogen vehicles.
questions.source: SSC CGL — calorific value definition and hydrogen having highest (~143 kJ/g) is underrated but important

questions.text: What is photochemical smog, and which gases are responsible for it?
options: Photochemical smog is fog that forms when factories burn coal; the 'photo' prefix means the smog is visible in photographs

Photochemical smog forms when nitrogen oxides (NOₓ) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from vehicle exhaust and industry react in the presence of sunlight to form ground-level ozone (O₃), aldehydes, peroxy acetyl nitrate (PAN), and brown NO₂. Causes eye/lung irritation, reduced visibility, and crop damage.
Photochemical smog is identical to industrial smog (London smog); both are caused by SO₂ from coal burning and water vapour condensing into acid fog
Photochemical smog is caused by CO₂ and methane reacting in sunlight; it is essentially the same as the greenhouse effect but concentrated near the ground
questions.explanation: Photochemical smog (also called Los Angeles smog or summer smog): Formation: Primary pollutants from vehicle exhaust: NOₓ (NO + NO₂) from combustion of fuels at high temperature (N₂ + O₂ → 2NO at engine temperatures → NO oxidises to NO₂ in air); VOCs (volatile organic compounds): unburnt hydrocarbons, aldehydes, from petrol evaporation, incomplete combustion, solvents, paints. Photochemical reactions (sunlight needed — hence 'photo'): NO₂ + UV → NO + O (atomic oxygen); O + O₂ → O₃ (ozone formed — the key photochemical smog component); O₃ + VOCs → aldehydes, PANs (Peroxyacyl Nitrates), radicals; Health effects: O₃: irritates lungs, causes asthma, chest pain; NO₂: brownish colour, lung irritant; Aldehydes (HCHO, CH₃CHO): eye irritation, lachrymatory (tear-inducing); PAN: eye irritant, damages plants. Agricultural damage: O₃ and PAN damage crops — estimated $10+ billion annually globally; Visible smog: brownish-yellow haze over cities (NO₂ is brown/reddish-brown). Contrast — Classical/London smog (winter smog): SO₂ + particulates + fog + cold temperature → sulfurous acid fog; Occurs in industrial cities burning sulphur-containing coal; Great London Smog (December 1952) killed 4,000–12,000 people in 4 days.
questions.source: SSC CGL — photochemical smog (NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight → O₃ + PANs) vs London smog is underrated but important

questions.text: What happens if you pass excess CO₂ into limewater that has already turned milky?
options: The milky solution becomes more turbid (cloudier) as more CaCO₃ is deposited
The milky solution clears (becomes transparent again) because excess CO₂ converts the insoluble CaCO₃ precipitate to soluble calcium bicarbonate [Ca(HCO₃)₂] which dissolves in water
The limewater turns blue when excess CO₂ is passed through it
Excess CO₂ causes the CaCO₃ to decompose back to Ca(OH)₂ and CO₂ — the original clear limewater is restored
questions.explanation: The two-stage limewater test — a classic SSC trap: STAGE 1 (limited CO₂): CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → CaCO₃↓ (white precipitate) + H₂O; Clear limewater → MILKY white. STAGE 2 (excess CO₂): CaCO₃ (insoluble) + CO₂ + H₂O → Ca(HCO₃)₂ (calcium bicarbonate, soluble); Milky white → CLEAR again; This clears because calcium bicarbonate is soluble in water — the precipitate dissolves. Applications — identifying CO₂: Confirm CO₂: bubble through limewater → milky. Confirm EXCESS CO₂: continue bubbling → milky clears → confirms CO₂ (not SO₂ or HCl which also cause similar initial cloudiness but don't clear on excess). Relevance in nature: River water dissolving limestone: CO₂ (from rain + air) + H₂O + CaCO₃ (limestone) → Ca(HCO₃)₂ (soluble) → limestone dissolves → forms caves (karst landscape); Stalactites/stalagmites: Ca(HCO₃)₂ → CaCO₃↓ + CO₂ + H₂O (as CO₂ escapes from dripping water in cave → CaCO₃ re-precipitates → forms calcium carbonate deposits → stalactites grow down, stalagmites grow up). This is also why temporary hard water (from Ca(HCO₃)₂) is removed by boiling — heating reverses the second reaction.
questions.source: SSC CGL — excess CO₂ clears milky limewater (CaCO₃ → Ca(HCO₃)₂) is underrated and a classic trap

questions.text: LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) is stored as a liquid in cylinders. Which gas is added to it to detect leaks?
options: Chlorine (Cl₂) — gives a sharp, disinfectant smell
Hydrogen sulphide (H₂S) — gives a rotten egg smell
Ethyl mercaptan (C₂H₅SH) or thiophane — gives a characteristic pungent smell
Ammonia (NH₃) — gives a sharp, acrid smell like cleaning fluid
questions.explanation: LPG (propane + butane) is naturally colourless and ODOURLESS — you cannot smell it unless an odorant is deliberately added. Odorant added: ETHYL MERCAPTAN (ethanethiol, C₂H₅SH) or THIOPHANE — sulphur-containing organic compounds with an intensely pungent, garlic-like or 'rotten cabbage' smell detectable at extremely low concentrations (1–10 ppm). Why mercaptans: Detectable at very low concentrations (human nose can smell < 1 ppm); Not removed by combustion (burns to SO₂ which is also detectable by smell); Chemically stable in LPG; Non-toxic at the concentrations used as odorant. Safety implications: When you smell 'gas' in kitchen → mercaptan smell → LPG leak; Immediately: DON'T switch on/off any electrical switches (sparks can ignite gas); Open windows and doors for ventilation; Move away from the area; Call the gas supplier. Cylinders: Red/maroon cylinders = domestic LPG (12.5 kg or 5 kg); Blue cylinders = commercial/industrial LPG. Important: H₂S (option B) smells like rotten eggs and is toxic, but is NOT added to LPG — it's a naturally occurring impurity in some natural gas deposits that is REMOVED before distribution, not added. NH₃ (option D) is added to industrial refrigerants to detect leaks but not to LPG.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL — ethyl mercaptan added to odourless LPG for leak detection is very frequently asked

questions.text: Which gas is produced in a biogas plant and what is the main raw material used?
options: Hydrogen gas; produced from water using solar energy
Methane (CH₄) is the main gas; produced by anaerobic (oxygen-free) digestion of organic waste — mainly cow dung (gobar), agricultural residue, and food waste by methanogenic bacteria
Carbon dioxide; produced from limestone by heating it with organic acids
Nitrogen gas; produced from the decomposition of protein-rich manure
questions.explanation: Biogas plant (gobar gas plant): Raw materials: Primarily cow dung (gobar) in India — hence 'gobar gas'; also used: agricultural residues, food waste, municipal solid waste, sewage sludge, algae, poultry droppings. Process — Anaerobic Digestion (AD): Stage 1 (Hydrolysis): Complex organic molecules (cellulose, proteins, fats) broken down by hydrolytic bacteria into simpler sugars, amino acids, fatty acids; Stage 2 (Acidogenesis): Acidogenic bacteria convert these to organic acids (acetic acid, propionic acid), H₂, CO₂; Stage 3 (Acetogenesis): Acetogenic bacteria convert organic acids to acetic acid, H₂, CO₂; Stage 4 (Methanogenesis): METHANOGENIC ARCHAEA (not bacteria!) convert acetic acid → CH₄ + CO₂; CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O (most important step). Biogas composition: CH₄: 50–65%; CO₂: 35–50%; H₂S, N₂, H₂O traces. Calorific value: ~22 MJ/m³ (lower than natural gas ~38 MJ/m³ because of CO₂ dilution). By-product: Slurry (digested sludge) — excellent organic fertiliser rich in N, P, K; safer than raw manure (pathogens killed in digester). When biogas is purified to >95% methane → 'biomethane' or 'Compressed Biogas (CBG)' — same as CNG, used in vehicles.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL — biogas = methane from anaerobic digestion of cow dung is very frequently asked

questions.text: Which among the following is NOT a greenhouse gas?
options: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
Methane (CH₄)
Nitrogen (N₂)
Nitrous oxide (N₂O)
questions.explanation: Nitrogen (N₂) is NOT a greenhouse gas — it is the most abundant gas in the atmosphere (78%) but does NOT cause any greenhouse effect. Greenhouse gases must ABSORB infrared (IR) radiation emitted by the Earth's warmed surface. Mechanism: Earth's surface absorbs solar radiation (visible light) → warms up → emits infrared radiation (heat); Greenhouse gases absorb this IR radiation → re-radiate in all directions → some returns to Earth's surface → warming ('greenhouse effect'). Why N₂ is NOT a greenhouse gas: N₂ is a symmetrical, homonuclear diatomic molecule (N≡N); It has NO dipole moment and no change in dipole moment when vibrating; IR photons cannot excite N₂ vibrational modes (IR-inactive); N₂ simply does not interact with IR radiation → passes through. Why CO₂, CH₄, H₂O, N₂O ARE greenhouse gases: These molecules have dipole moments or change in dipole moment when vibrating → interact with IR radiation → absorb it → heat up → re-emit → trap heat. Similarly, O₂ (diatomic, symmetric) is also NOT a greenhouse gas for the same reason as N₂. All the major greenhouse gases: CO₂ (GWP=1), CH₄ (GWP≈28), N₂O (GWP≈273), H₂O vapour (most abundant GHG by mass), HFCs, CFCs, SF₆, O₃ (tropospheric).
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL — nitrogen (N₂) is NOT a greenhouse gas (homonuclear, no dipole) is very frequently asked

questions.text: Which gas is used to fill weather balloons and airships instead of hydrogen, and why?
options: Nitrogen — because it is cheaper than hydrogen and lighter than air
Helium — because it provides good lift (second lightest gas) while being completely non-flammable, unlike hydrogen which is highly flammable (Hindenburg disaster, 1937)
Argon — because it is a noble gas that doesn't react with atmospheric moisture at high altitude
Carbon dioxide — because it maintains the balloon shape at high altitudes where pressure is very low
questions.explanation: Helium (He) replaced hydrogen in balloons and airships: Hydrogen for lift: H₂ is the lightest gas (density 0.09 g/L vs air 1.29 g/L) → excellent lift; BUT H₂ is highly flammable (burns in air between 4–75% concentration — an exceptionally wide flammability range). The Hindenburg disaster (6 May 1937): German airship LZ 129 Hindenburg → caught fire while mooring in Lakehurst, NJ, USA; Burned in 37 seconds; 35 of 97 people on board died + 1 ground worker; Ended the era of hydrogen-filled passenger airships. Helium for safety: Density = 0.18 g/L (heavier than H₂ but still much lighter than air → provides good lift: ~93% of H₂'s lift); Completely non-flammable (noble gas — zero combustibility); Chemical formula: He (monatomic, Group 18). Uses of helium balloons: Weather balloons — radiosonde balloons measuring atmospheric temperature, pressure, humidity at altitude (carried by He balloons); Airships/blimps (advertising, surveillance); Scientific research balloons; Party balloons and decorative balloons. Sources: Helium is extracted from natural gas deposits (where He is trapped underground — produced by radioactive decay of uranium and thorium); Primarily from USA and Qatar; Helium cannot be produced chemically or synthetically — it's a finite, non-renewable resource.
questions.source: SSC CGL / SSC CHSL — helium used in balloons (non-flammable, vs hydrogen Hindenburg disaster) is very frequently asked
